west-indies-christmas-a-mixtape.title: West Indies Christmas (A Mixtape)
west-indies-christmas-a-mixtape.author: Justin Gage
west-indies-christmas-a-mixtape.description: The holidays, West Indies style. With the expansion of record distribution from the UK, the US, and Canada during the 1960s, Christmas music experienced a notable surge, mirroring developments in Jamaica. The following twenty track all vinyl mixtape covers most of that time period, from the early 60s through the late 70s.
west-indies-christmas-a-mixtape.body_text:
The holidays, West Indies style. With the expansion of record distribution from the UK, the US, and Canada during the 1960s, Christmas music experienced a notable surge, mirroring developments in Jamaica. The following twenty track all vinyl mixtape covers most of that time period, from early the 60s through the late 70s.

Jamaica was quick to catch on. Figures like Byron Lee became connectors, scouting talent from Barbados and Trinidad & Tobago and bringing them to Kingston to record and collaborate. The Opels were one such discovery. Kalyan even found themselves opening for The Commodores and the Jackson 5, while Boo & The Tru-Tones landed a surprise hit with their cover of Hot Chocolate’s “You Sexy Thing,” later taking the stage at the Super Bowl XIII halftime show in 1979 for a Carnival-themed performance. Wild Fire—originally known as The Sparks—rounded out the scene. Many of the labels behind this music—WIRL, Trex—operated out of Kingston, even as their catalogs leaned heavily toward Barbados and Trinidad & Tobago. | Ryan Baxley

the-joys-of-cookin-tracks-from-groove-me.title: The Joys of Cookin’: Tracks from Groove Merchant, 1971-1974 (A Mixtape)
the-joys-of-cookin-tracks-from-groove-me.album: The Joys of Cookin’: Tracks from Groove Merchant, 1971-1974 (A Mixtape)
the-joys-of-cookin-tracks-from-groove-me.author: Justin Gage
the-joys-of-cookin-tracks-from-groove-me.description: Founded in 1971, Sonny Lester's Groove Merchant label was pretty far removed from what was going on in jazz at the beginning of that decade. Fusion, black power, spiritual jazz and ECM were all happening elsewhere. Maybe that's what makes it so much fun. Groove Merchant was content to serve up sweaty, funky party jazz, brimming with flute blowouts, wicked guitar and miles of Hammond B3 soul. Thrown in some softcore cover art, and you've got something cooking. Lester's short-lived label had grooves to spare. Call it a case of truth in advertising.
the-joys-of-cookin-tracks-from-groove-me.body_text:
The Groove Merchant label has become such a revered name in cratedigging and acid jazz circles that it is difficult to remember its somewhat hokey pedigree. Label founder Sonny Lester was an old A&R man whose name had primarily been associated with easy listening schlock like Those Fantabulous Strings and novelty burlesque records like Ann Corio’s How to Strip For Your Husband. Lester was the bandleader on a nifty exotica record After Hours Middle East that still gets some love from connoisseurs, but no one would have mistaken him—unlike his friend Bob Thiele—as a man with his finger on the pulse of modern jazz. Lester’s mid-to-late 60s outfit Solid State Records earned some credibility with early releases by Chick Corea, but it mostly stuck to more mass appeal fare like big band records and the popular soul jazz of Hammond B3 preachers like Jimmy McGriff. Solid State’s successor Groove Merchant, founded in 1971, seemed no less interested in chasing hits and pleasing crowds. The grooves got thicker, to be sure, but the listening was still pretty easy.

There was a revolution upending jazz, but nobody bothered to tell Groove Merchant. Nothing on the label really resembles what was happening to jazz in 1971. The year of Live-Evil, Mwandishi, Journey in Satchidananda, Escalator over the Hill, Weather Report and The Inner Mounting Flame might as well have been taking place in another dimension. Only Chick Corea’s pensive Sundance (with Dave Holland, Bennie Maupin, Woody Shaw and Jack DeJohnette) seems like it is breathing the same atmosphere. And pianist Larry Willis’s ferocious Inner Crisis from 1973 points toward the politically conscious deep jazz indie labels of the day like Strata-East and Tribe. Those aside, Lester mostly stuck with bread-and-butter soul jazz: organ combos cranking out pop covers; funky crossover guitarists; wicked flute blowouts; a smattering of jazz standards given the good-time treatment.

This is the irony of the late twentieth century cult of Groove Merchant among cratediggers and sample-hunters: it was already something of a throwback in its own day. They were going retro for a sound that was already retro. Most of its releases (hell, many of its artists) spiritually belonged to the early 1960s—before fusion, before spiritual jazz, before black power, before ECM.

Ultimately, Groove Merchant was a conservative project: it served up sweaty, funky old school party jazz to a world that had largely moved on to more immersive, mind-expanding, consciousness-raising material. Just look at the label’s often outrageously risqué cover art: Groove Merchant fucked. You could go chase revolution and enlightenment elsewhere. It is no coincidence that the label’s sound was rediscovered and championed in the late 1980s acid jazz heyday, when the reputation of 70s psychedelic jazz excess was probably at its nadir.

Fortunately, we don’t have to choose between those moments. Aquarium Drunkard has been a tireless evangelist for those other faces of the 1970s revolution in jazz. Today, we thought you could use a little get-down. | b sirota

radio-free-heaven-a-mix-inspired-by-phil.title: Radio Free Heaven: A Mix Inspired By Philip K. Dick and The Transmigration of Timothy Archer
radio-free-heaven-a-mix-inspired-by-phil.album: Radio Free Heaven: A Mix Inspired By Philip K. Dick and The Transmigration of Timothy Archer
radio-free-heaven-a-mix-inspired-by-phil.author: Justin Gage
radio-free-heaven-a-mix-inspired-by-phil.description: The Transmigration of Timothy Archer, Philip K. Dick’s underappreciated final novel, is framed by death. It begins with a singularly famous death—the assassination of John Lennon in 1980—and follows the reverberations of that event backward in time to the San Francisco Bay Area of the late 60s and 70s, where Episcopal Bishop Timothy Archer is in the midst of a crisis of faith. The book is a characteristically Dick-ian smoothie of thematic contradictions: sacred/popular, surreal/mundane, historical/fictional, faith/gnosis.

tags.name: Dig into the AD mixtape archives spanning the past twenty years . . .

radio-free-heaven-a-mix-inspired-by-phil.body_text:
The Transmigration of Timothy Archer, Philip K. Dick’s underappreciated final novel, is framed by death. It begins with a singularly famous death—the assassination of John Lennon in 1980—and follows the reverberations of that event backward in time to the San Francisco Bay Area of the late 60s and 70s, where Episcopal Bishop Timothy Archer is in the midst of a crisis of faith.

Archer (based on real-life Bishop James Pike), we learn, also dies, in his case after venturing into a desert in Palestine trying to find God, whom he believed to be a sacred hallucinogenic species of mushroom. Archer’s death and the psychic transduction of his life determine the events of the rest of the book. Transmigration is a characteristically Dick-ian smoothie of thematic contradictions: sacred/popular, surreal/mundane, historical/fictional, faith/gnosis. Topically it spans Christian historiography, psychedelics, the rise of West Coast Woo and New Age spiritualism, the German Enlightenment, psychiatry and neurodivergence, and the metamorphosis of the 60s.

Radio Free Heaven is a loose adaptation of the novel into the medium of the mixtape. As Transmigration is fundamentally inseparable from its specific time and place, the following mix is focused on contemporaneous music, Bay Area artists, and topical archival recordings, but I also broke that rule once or twice when it was called for. Thank you for listening and, as Angel Archer says: have fun with your thoughts. | Patrick Ellis

Radio Free Heaven: A Mix Inspired By Philip K. Dick and The Transmigration of Timothy Archer

first-last-angura-a-mixtape.title: First & Last: Angura (A Mixtape)
first-last-angura-a-mixtape.author: Justin Gage
first-last-angura-a-mixtape.description: An introduction to Japanese folk in 1970s Japan, the following mix was created as a companion to the article on Modern Bible and the story of Gekidan Buraiha. It brings together a selection of early Japanese folk, protest songs and Angura recordings from the same era, providing additional context and atmosphere for the world the little theatre movement troupe emerged from.
first-last-angura-a-mixtape.body_text:
An introduction to Japanese folk in 1970s Japan, the following mix was created as a companion to the article on Modern Bible and the story of Gekidan Buraiha. It brings together a selection of early Japanese folk, protest songs and Angura recordings from the same era, providing additional context and atmosphere for the world the little theatre movement troupe emerged from.

First & Last: Angura (A Mixtape)

Wataru Takada – 自衛隊に入ろう = Join the Self-Defense Forces (1969): This is based on “Andorra,” an anti-war song released by Pete Seeger with lyrics containing paradoxical irony satirizing the Japanese Self-Defense Forces. However, when someone took the lyrics literally and actually enlisted in the Self-Defense Forces, Takada decided to retire the song.

Kan Mikami – 夢は夜ひらく = My Dreams Bloom at Night (1970): Kan Mikami started out his career on Columbia Records where he struggled because his songs kept getting banned from broadcasting. Kan Mikami later performed with Shuji Terayama’s theatre troupe Tenjo Sajiki.

J. A. Seazer – すべての人が死んで行く時に = When Everybody’s Going To Die (1970): The first recording by J. A. Seazer, released in 1970 when he joined Tenjo Sajiki.

Juro Kara – さすらいの唄 = The Wanderer’s Song (1970): Debut single by avant-garde playwright and theatre director Juro Kara released on King Records. Music and backing band by Hitoshi Komuro and his group Rokumonsen, of URC fame.

Tokyo Kid Brothers – アメリカの夢 = The American Dream (1971): Live recording of the theatre troupe Tokyo Kid Brothers, which may have been recorded at Café La MaMa in New York, where they performed in 1970. They later established a La MaMa in Tokyo, which does not appear to have had any official connection with Ellen Stewart’s Off-Off-Broadway venue other than the name.

J. A. Seazer & Tenjo Sajiki – 和讃 = Hymn (1973): Probably needs no introduction, one of the most known and beloved psychedelic records to ever come out of Japan.

Gekidan Buraiha – 泳ぎきれない重い雨 = A Heavy Rain That You Cannot Swim Through (1975): The most obscure and unknown of the angura records. Sung by actor Toru Okamoto. The song’s lyrics convey a sense of disillusionment with political activism, disappointment with student protests, resistance to becoming a cog in the machine of consumerism, and an overall sense of powerlessness shared among the aforementioned youths in the mid-1970s.

Kyokubakan – 旅の役者で御座居ます = I Am A Traveling Performer (1978): Another relatively unknown angura troupe. This self-released record is rumored to have had most of its copies destroyed in a fire.

Juro Kara & Jokyo Gekidan – ジョンシルバーの合唱 = John Silver’s Chorus (1977): Live recording from 1973 released on Bellwood Records circa 1977 from one of Juro Kara’s most famous plays, John Silver: The Beggar of Love.

Yosuke Yamashita Trio & Dairakudakan – ゴースト パートII = Ghosts Part II( 1977): A bit of a bonus track. Buraiha’s lead actor, Mr. Takahashi, became a Butoh dancer shortly after leaving the troupe. This underrated record by the Yosuke Yamashita Trio features Dairakudakan, one of the first Butoh dance troupes, founded in 1972. I haven’t asked Mr. Takahashi if they were an inspiration, but I would not be surprised to hear that they were.

 

 
 Posted on October 9, 2025October 8, 2025First & Last Japan Mixtapes

1981-a-post-punk-compilation.title: 1981 :: A Post-Punk Compilation
1981-a-post-punk-compilation.author: Justin Gage
1981-a-post-punk-compilation.description: Long live the old, weird internet. Out of all the genres, post-punk has always felt like the hardest to pin down. What the hell was / is it? I’m not sure I could tell you. But I can tell you that the Musicophilia blog’s massive 1981 box set of mixes is perhaps the best representation of the wild burst of creativity that was happening in the underground at the time. While the original mp3 downloads of the box set have since been replaced at Musicophilia with streaming options, we have uploaded the original files for posterity ...
1981-a-post-punk-compilation.body_text:
Out of all the genres, post-punk has always felt like the hardest to pin down. What the hell was / is it? I’m not sure I could tell you. But I can tell you that the Musicophilia blog’s massive 1981 box set of mixes is perhaps the best representation of the wild burst of creativity that was happening in the underground at the time. It’s a kaleidoscope of strange and dazzling sounds, all put together with a connoisseur’s ear and a fan’s heart. 1981 compiler Ian Manire writes: “If this set has a thesis, it’s that post-punk was defined not by its ‘greats,’ but by how great even its peripheral players were, as a movement that empowered people to both take over the means of production (and distribution and criticism and celebration, in ways that epitomized the ethos of ‘independent music’ to this day), and also the means of expression. It democratized artiness and attitude. You didn’t have to be spiky and energetic or arty and erudite: why not be both? And you didn’t need anyone’s permission.”

Originally a lovely handmade physical item that first emerged via message boards back in the mid-2000s, 1981 has since joined a host of other expertly curated Musicophilia mixes in the online realm. However you hear it, you’re in for a treat. Go ahead and dial into another frequency. | t wilcox

Download: 1981 :: A Post-Punk Compilation (1.6 GB Zipped Folder)

Note: the original mp3 downloads of the 1981 box set have since been replaced at Musicophilia with streaming options. We have uploaded the original files for posterity via the above link (and recommend you burn them to CD-R). Also, be sure to head over to the Musicophilia site to read Manire’s 2004 notes on the project’s history and intention. Long live the old, weird internet.

 

 
 Posted on September 26, 2025September 25, 2025Compilation Mixtapes Post-Punk

l-a-burnout-volumes-one-two.description: Via 2006, back by subscriber request as Autumn approaches. L.A. Burnout is made up of the sounds floating around Los Angeles in the late ’60s and ’70s. A faded aural imprint of the canyons, beaches, wildfires, neighborhoods, late-nights and early mornings of a bygone L.A. Play this mix around dusk while driving through Topanga with the windows rolled down – it’s the next best thing to time travel.
l-a-burnout-volumes-one-two.body_text:
The original 2006 downloads of L.A. Burnout. Back by subscriber request as Autumn approaches.

L.A. Burnout is made up of the sounds floating around Los Angeles in the late ’60s and ’70s. A faded aural imprint of the canyons, beaches, wildfires, neighborhoods, late-nights and early mornings of a bygone L.A. Play this mix around dusk while driving through Topanga with the windows rolled down – it’s the next best thing to time travel.

double-wide-denim-a-medley.title: Double Wide Denim: A Medley
double-wide-denim-a-medley.author: Justin Gage
double-wide-denim-a-medley.description: Double Wide Denim: a weird amalgam of Mel McDaniel, Don Williams, Dickey Betts, David Allan Coe, Molly Hatchet, ZZ Top, Dwight Yoakam and beyond. A playlist that evokes Coors Lite, charcoal grills, ice chests, lakes and pontoon boats.
double-wide-denim-a-medley.body_text:
Music’s where you find it. I spent the last two summers of high school working at a warehouse off I-285 in Atlanta. Within two weeks I was the forklift operator which, at 16, was much cooler than my back-of-house restaurant gig the previous summer. I was the only kid on the floor, the other guys were roughneck dudes in their early thirties. The manager, a middle-aged guy named Skip, mainly sat in his air-conditioned office reading the New York Times while listening to the score to Kubrick’s 2001: A Space Odyssey on compact disc. He rarely bothered us. The floor boss, a proud Busch swilling redneck named Dodge, ran the actual show. Dodge looked straight out of central casting for a bar fight, commuted in from Doraville, chewed Levi Garrett straight from the pouch, and would regale my young mind with tales of tree stands, cleaning deer and chasing women.

There were three other guys on the floor: an affable born-again acid casualty who had dropped out of the Lovett school at age 17 to follow the Grateful Dead for 10 years, a KISS obsessed ex-Marine, and a strange mustachioed misanthrope. But, like I said, it was Dodge’s show and Dodge’s rules, and one of those rules had to do with the floor’s radio protocol and it was unbendable: every other day would alternate between the local FM classic rock and country stations. As Walkmans were not permitted, I would have to listen to Pavement and A Tribe Called Quest on my own time.

I worked in that hot and humid 20,000 square foot space for about six months between 1993 and 1994. This was my baptism in modern country and southern rock as dictated by the commercial radio of the day, and while I was not ready to trade in my green Adidas Gazelles for a pair of Ropers, I began to get a feel for it. Growing up in the south, country music was as common as the ambient cacophony of junebugs and cicadas, but this was the first time I had paid much, if any, attention to it.

A few summers ago I began compiling a playlist of what those long, muggy days sounded like. The result, a weird amalgam of Mel McDaniel, David Allan Coe, Molly Hatchet, Dickey Betts, Don Williams, ZZ Top and beyond, is Double Wide Denim — a tracklisting that evokes Coors Lite, charcoal grills, ice chests, lakes and pontoon boats.

up-in-my-mind-an-august-mixtape.title: Up In My Mind: An August Mixtape
up-in-my-mind-an-august-mixtape.album: Up In My Mind: An August Mixtape
up-in-my-mind-an-august-mixtape.author: Justin Gage
up-in-my-mind-an-august-mixtape.description: One hundred and twenty-seven minutes of strange and mercurial music – slow burning, sprawling, smoggy, and ephemeral. 60s Kenyan folk and Thai garage rock; late 70s drum machine gospel from Inkster, Michigan, and private press psych from the Pacific Northwest; mid 80s Congolese electronic soul and Senegalese art-funk; Zambian highlife circa 1991 and experimental computer music made in a juvenile detention center in modern day Albuquerque. The same Hawa Daisy Moore mp3 files that were used in the inaugural Blue August Moon eleven years ago – the crackling tropical oasis showing signs of increased deterioration. These are just some of the sounds that Up In My Mind—the latest edition of our August mixtape—is steeped in. An irregular late summer tradition, but ever an occasion to look up, zoom in, and zone out.
up-in-my-mind-an-august-mixtape.body_text:

youre-futures-in-space-eddie-harris-on-a.title: You’re Future’s In Space: Eddie Harris on Atlantic (A Mixtape)
youre-futures-in-space-eddie-harris-on-a.album: You’re Future’s In Space: Eddie Harris on Atlantic (A Mixtape)
youre-futures-in-space-eddie-harris-on-a.author: Justin Gage
youre-futures-in-space-eddie-harris-on-a.description: From the late 60s through the mid-70s, Eddie Harris indulged in a string of progressively freakier, beautifully executed records that smeared boundaries, blew minds and sold poorly. This mixtape collects two and a half hours of the most adventurous moments from those heady Atlantic days.
youre-futures-in-space-eddie-harris-on-a.body_text:
At the close of the 1960s, Eddie Harris had earned himself carte blanche at Atlantic records. Miles Davis immortalized Harris’ composition, “Freedom Jazz Dance,” on 1967’s Miles Smiles, his early adoption of electronics on LPs like The Electrifying Eddie Harris and Plug Me In put him firmly atop the jazz and R&B charts, and an unrehearsed collab with Les McCann at the 1969 Montreux Jazz Fest, Swiss Movement, moved millions.

For Harris, the artistic freedom couldn’t have arrived at a better time. Ever ahead of the curve, his use of the electric piano and amplified horn beginning in the mid 60s had flowered into full-blown tape delay drenched psychedelia by 1968’s Silver Cycles. Though, while Miles, McCann and even Harris’ own bassist and compositional partner, Melvin Jackson, quickly indulged in album-length headiness (see McCann’s Invitation to Openness, and Layers, or Jackson’s Funky Skull for proof), Harris’ transition began timidly – balancing his typically left-of-center post bop with a whiff or two of stoned freakiness within every LP.

By the mid-70s, that eccentricity went full tilt with Harris’ joyous experimentation spread across both sides of the record. 1974’s Is It In was a freak-fest of drum machines, octave divided horns, chanted vocals and deep, repetitive grooves. A Prince joint that pre-empted the Purple One by nearly half a decade. I Need Some Money doubled down, introduced Harris as a vocalist, and picked up where Sly’s Fresh and Small Talk left off – tighter arrangements, stranger sounds, deeper zones. As he told Soul Train’s Don Cornelius in 1975, “I’m indulging into the vocalizations nowadays because everyone else seems to think they can sing, so I might as well join em.”

1975’s Bad Luck Is All I Have is the masterwork. Expertly performed on an arsenal of hybrid instruments (the guitorgan, Fender’s novel bass VI, brass horns fitted with reed mouthpieces), its six tracks are planets from the furthest corners of Harris’ strange universe – its sun, the gorgeous “Why Must We Part”, with slowly building, endlessly layered vocal harmonies, string synth and delicate, subtly mind-blowing bass.

Middling sales be damned, Harris recorded a few more discs for Atlantic, including two sides of raunchy stage banter collected as The Reason Why I’m Talking S–t, and while they included moments of brilliance, none burned as hot as his previous work.

This mixtape collects two and a half hours of the most adventurous moments from those heady Atlantic days. Sequenced chronologically, building slowly, flowering and flaming out, it mirrors the fascinating journey of one of the music’s most misunderstood eccentrics. | j erwin

You’re Future’s In Space: Eddie Harris on Atlantic (Zipped Folder)

Includes selections from: Silver Cycles (1968), High Voltage [live] (1969), Free Speech (1969), Come on Down! (1970), Instant Death (1971), Eddie Harris Sings the Blues (1972), Excursions (1973), E.H. in the U.K. (1974), Is It In (1974), I Need Some Money (1974), Bad Luck Is All I Have (1975), That Is Why You’re Overweight (1975)

dubwise-summer-an-aquarium-drunkard-mixt.title: Dubwise Summer: An Aquarium Drunkard Mixtape
dubwise-summer-an-aquarium-drunkard-mixt.album: Dubwise Summer: An Aquarium Drunkard Mixtape
dubwise-summer-an-aquarium-drunkard-mixt.author: Justin Gage
dubwise-summer-an-aquarium-drunkard-mixt.description: Summer equals dub. It is the elemental music of the season. And not simply because it comes from a tropical island. Everything in dub feels suspended in the heat and humidity: a little slower, a little hazier—all the elements delayed in the muggy atmosphere. There is something about the laid back languor of dub that feels especially appropriate this time of year, when the sun is high and the stakes are low. In that spirit, we're serving up a couple hours of some of our favorite cuts from the golden age of dub.

tags.name: Dig into twenty years of AD mixtapes via the archives …

dubwise-summer-an-aquarium-drunkard-mixt.body_text:
Summer equals dub. It is the elemental music of the season. The tropical island birthplace of dub boasts an average annual temperature in the low eighties; and the genre’s pioneers managed to infuse that pervasive mugginess into every inch of the sound. Everything in dub feels suspended in the high humidity: a little slower than usual; a little hazier—delayed in the atmosphere. The rattlesnake bass, snatches of passerby vocals, smears of horn, the mirage of melodica, the easy guitar skank rising and falling like a welcome breeze off the water, and those beats battering down like the sun itself. All of it slightly warped and shimmering in the noonday heat. Dub belongs to the warm weather. Perhaps this is why those of us who aren’t in Jamaica find ourselves reaching for it during the summer months. There is something about the languor and low stakes of dub that feels especially appropriate this time of year. It can punch without getting too aggro; it can bewitch without getting too psychedelic. It is open-ended, but not without structure and logic. As head music goes, it is toasted, rather than hallucinogenic. Dub makes us move, but too fast, and not too much. That beautifully blunted, medium-tempo momentum might be all anyone needs in this weather. In that spirit, here are a couple hours of some choice riddims from the golden age of dub. Stay positive, stay hydrated and enjoy. | b sirota

mood-stabilizers-the-post-rock-era-1993-.title: Mood Stabilizers: The Post-Rock Era, 1993-2002 (A Mixtape)
mood-stabilizers-the-post-rock-era-1993-.album: Mood Stabilizers: The Post-Rock Era, 1993-2002 (A Mixtape)
mood-stabilizers-the-post-rock-era-1993-.author: Justin Gage
mood-stabilizers-the-post-rock-era-1993-.description: We've had post-rock on the brain lately. New music from genre pioneers Tortoise and the prospect of a North American tour by Stereolab this fall, not to mention recent archival releases by the likes of Aerial M, Gastr del Sol and Seefeel have many of us revisiting the sound's 1990s salad days. For those wanting more, we've compiled a mixtape of some three hours of the genre's knottiest groove logistics. For the uninitiated, here's a crash course in what the end of the twentieth century sounded like.

tags.name: Dig into twenty years of AD mixtapes via the archives …

mood-stabilizers-the-post-rock-era-1993-.body_text:
People forget that Francis Fukuyama ended his famous summer 1989 rumination on the “end of history” on a note of dejection. The communist Eastern Bloc was falling apart and the Berlin Wall would be down before the calendar year was up. Fukuyama, a political scientist and analyst at the State Department, surmised in the manifest end of the Cold War what he described as “the total exhaustion of viable systematic alternatives to Western liberalism” and wondered whether it could be said that history had, in some sense, reached its terminal point. He did not mean, of course, that nothing would ever happen again. Rather, he meant that with the fiery destruction of fascism in the first half of the twentieth century and the apparent wreck of communism happening all around him, that humanity had at last found its final social and political form in the liberal democratic capitalism of the Western world. The only option left was a society characterized by, in his words, “liberal democracy in the political sphere combined with easy access to VCRs and stereos in the economic.” And over this he could not help but lament. “The end of history will be a very sad time,” Fukuyama wrote. A century of global ideological struggle, of idealism and heroism and utopian aspiration, would give way to an age of technical problem-solving, environmental concerns and the endless satisfaction of consumer demand.

More than any other musical form, post-rock was what the end of history actually sounded like. The ascendancy of the punk, noise and sludge sounds that eventually got tagged as grunge was really the harvest of college rock seeds planted at the tail-end of the Reagan-Bush era. Its studied disaffections and alienations made sense as a repudiation of 1980s conservatism and materialist excess, but they persisted in the 1990s as more of a hangover (and later, an affectation) than anything else. The same might be said, mutatis mutandis, of rave in the UK, whose liberatory imperatives seem inseparable from Thatcherism. But post-rock belonged fully to what we might call the high 90s, the Clinton era; and even while emerging from the interstices of alternative and electronica, it was emotionally an altogether different species. Post-rock permitted neither wallowing nor ecstatic release, content instead to burble along at a bemused medium-cool.

In post-rock, the anhedonia of the end of history found its ideal form. Indeed, post-rock was often conventionally inexpressive by design, studiously uninterested in trafficking in the kinds of human feelings that make up the traditional material of pop music. Love songs, road songs, revolutions, memories, teenage symphonies to God—all of them were reduced to the technical problem-solving that Fukuyama had projected as the ethos of the coming era. When the track starts skipping around minute fourteen of Chicago band Tortoise’s genre-defining epic, “Djed,” it isn’t a comment on the artificiality of recorded sound; it is just another sonic impasse to work around. The skipping simply serves as a new click track, and the groove is painstakingly reassembled atop the malfunction. Art became, in some sense, a matter of logistics.

Literary theorist Fredric Jameson once argued that postmodern art was characterized, above all, by what he described as “depthlessness,” by which he didn’t mean shallowness in a pejorative sense, but rather superficiality in a perfectly literal sense: a culture consumed with the play of surfaces—gleaming, reflective, transparent, ornate. This accounts, in some measure, for post-rock’s origami-like sonic constructions: surfaces folded meticulously into form. Even at its most portentous—say, in the so-called ‘crescendo-core’ music that evolved out of Mogwai and Godspeed You! Black Emperor—post-rock was never really transcendental ‘head music’ in the sense of psychedelia or spiritual jazz. It was a music of filigree and figuration. Like any good postmodernist, it didn’t want to be accused of selling anybody a bill of goods.

The basic building-blocks of the form were krautrock, dub, IDM, shoegaze and lounge. But post-rock’s true signature was repetition—and this it had gleaned above all from the American minimalists. Indeed, one could argue that post-rock aimed at the integration of Reich and Glass into a rock idiom the way their German forebears in Can had attempted to integrate Stockhausen and Ligeti a generation earlier. Post-rock was often, as Philip Glass describes his own work, “music with repetitive structures”—only now set to a backbeat. As much as its postmodern eclecticism, this consuming interest in repetition and reiteration is what made the genre so appropriate to its historical moment. Rock musicians in the 1960s wrote songs about traveling, about the romance of the open road. Post-rockers, by contrast, opted for the steady state: the music moved, but it seldom progressed. There was no place to go, because one place was pretty much the same as the next. Surely it was no accident that Glenn Jones named his crucial Boston post-rock outfit Cul de Sac.

Post-rock has been on people’s minds lately. In March, genre godfathers Tortoise released their first new music in over a decade. Stereolab recently announced that it will be touring North America in the fall. Archival collections of founding Tortoise guitarist Dave Pajo’s Aerial M project and David Grubbs and Jim O’Rourke’s formative Gastr del Sol have invited fresh reappraisals of the importance of their work. The recent reissue of early UK post-rockers Seefeel’s gleaming classic Quique has reminded listeners of the allure of this type of steady-state groove. And, of course, Tortoise and Isotope 217 guitarist Jeff Parker’s decamping for the west coast some years back has turned the adventurous Los Angeles jazz scene into a veritable colony of the late twentieth-century Chicago sound. In the outstanding work of Parker’s ETA IVtet bandmates bassist Anna Butters and saxophonist Josh Johnson, you can hear the legacy of post-rock being worked out in real time.

Of course, the polycrisis of American life in the 2020s surely plays no small part in nostalgia for the 1990s. The vaunted ‘end of history’ proved no greater than a brief parenthesis, stretching from the end of the Cold War to the events of September 11, 2001, and our culture has hardly ceased being throttled since its resumption. Seems as good an opportunity as any for a time-capsule of that fleeting moment in music history. Upon revisiting the sound of 90s post-rock, it seems strange to envy it its detachment and aimlessness, the almost-complacency with which it was content to stand still. But when the present alternatives are terror and dread, one can hardly gainsay the appeal. | b sirota

pangaea-deluxe-live-miles-in-japan-1975-.title: Pangaea Deluxe: Live Miles in Japan, 1975 (Mixtape)
pangaea-deluxe-live-miles-in-japan-1975-.album: Pangaea Deluxe: Live Miles in Japan, 1975 (Mixtape)
pangaea-deluxe-live-miles-in-japan-1975-.author: Justin Gage
pangaea-deluxe-live-miles-in-japan-1975-.description: Drifting from spiraling psychedelia into frightening soundscapes and ecstatic funk, the Miles Davis septet built a universe unto itself across its 1975 tour of Japan. This tour-spanning mix gathers some of the choicest cuts and most experimental moments from the unissued tapes.
pangaea-deluxe-live-miles-in-japan-1975-.body_text:
Capturing sets that slowly drift from sprawling psychedelia and ecstatic funk into punishing synth noise and ambient soundscapes, tapes of the Miles Davis septet’s 1975 tour of Japan reveal a band hell bent on terraforming its own universe. This 2-hour companion mix gathers the finest, most far-reaching moments from the circulating tapes along with a deep whiff of heady experimentation that was edited from the officially released concert recordings.

It’s easy to draw parallels between the more sinister elements of this music, of which there are many, and Miles’ personal descent into the murk – a spiral of physical and mental deterioration that ended in self-imposed exile just eight months later. Don’t fall for it. This is the muffled roar of a new world taking shape. | j erwin

Pangaea Deluxe: Live Miles in Japan, 1975 (Mixtape)

lamentations-twenty-two-songs-about-john.title: Lamentations: Twenty-Two Songs about John Coltrane
lamentations-twenty-two-songs-about-john.album: Lamentations: Twenty-Two Songs about John Coltrane
lamentations-twenty-two-songs-about-john.author: Justin Gage
lamentations-twenty-two-songs-about-john.description: In the nearly six decades since his untimely passing, musicians from all over the world have never stopped honoring John Coltrane. And not just artists in the jazz tradition, those in rock, funk, prog and soul as well. We put together a compilation of twenty-two of our favorite tributes to the visionary saxophonist. In the extraordinary variety of ways musicians have chosen to honor him, you can see an outline of the magnitude of his impact on modern music.
lamentations-twenty-two-songs-about-john.body_text:
No American artist has inspired as much music as John Coltrane. His colossal stature in jazz, his mentorship of an entire generation of players, the overtly spiritual import of his later works, and his early death at age forty from liver cancer all together practically conferred sainthood on the saxophonist almost immediately after his passing in 1967. Of course, composers were penning tributes to Coltrane while he was still with us on this plane—tracks like Grant Green’s wonderful “Talkin’ about J.C.” The irresistible homonym of his nickname in tracks like Duke Ellington’s “Take the Coltrane” or Woody Shaw’s “The Moontrane,” served as an easy shorthand for the commendation. But since his death, John Coltrane has haunted modern music. 

The better part of whole careers in late twentieth-century jazz were subsequently spent working through the uncompromising immensity of his musical legacy. And more than a few of his disciples eventually had to call upon him by name, like the invocation of a saint, as much as in prayer as in memoriam. Indeed, one could claim the whole of what would come to be called ‘spiritual jazz’ was but a set of footnotes to Coltrane. But what is remarkable is how far beyond jazz the cult has spread. Prog weirdos like Magma were as forthright in their debts as soul poets like Gil Scott-Heron.  Steve Hillage and Miquette Giraudy’s post-Gong electronic chill unit System 7 found themselves speaking his name. Even the apex 1980s Paisley Underground outfit The Dream Syndicate knew that a bottle of wine in the freezer and “some John Coltrane on the stereo” was a solid plan for getting some. (Or “just play me John Coltrane,” as Lucinda Williams once snarled.) And hip-hop namechecks of the saxophonist would probably add another hour to this mix. But we focused here on the expansiveness John Coltrane bequeathed to music. Some of the cuts assembled here climb way into double-digit run times as if honoring the saint in anything less would be more than a little indecorous. Together they make an almost-cubist portrait of Coltrane’s legacy, with various planes of his vision and influence superimposed upon one another at odd angles. It is no substitute for listening to the man himself. But it begins at least to outline the magnitude of his absence. | b sirota

grey-on-gray-ii-a-december-mixtape.title: Grey On Gray II :: A December Mixtape
grey-on-gray-ii-a-december-mixtape.author: Justin Gage
grey-on-gray-ii-a-december-mixtape.description: For the darkest time of year, a wintry mix of stark sonic landscapes — jazz, ambient, acoustic and unclassifiable. Instrumental treks through the snow, hopefully with a mug of hot chocolate (or something stronger) awaiting you at your final destination.
grey-on-gray-ii-a-december-mixtape.body_text:

first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-14.title: First & Last: Japanese Private Press, Vol. 14
first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-14.album: First & Last: Japanese Private Press, Vol. 14
first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-14.author: Justin Gage
first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-14.description: Step into spring with the latest edition of First & Last, a series of mixes providing a glimpse into the world of Japanese private press, or 自主盤, pronounced “jishuban”, which loosely translates to “independent board.” Included are tracks from two of our favorite records: Book End, whose band leader was also the leader of Primary Mini-Band, and Naotune Saga. The mix also features Buraiha, a band steeped in the avant-garde Angura movement, along with a group called Sharons Forever which (with no liner notes to guide) offers a poignant tribute, leaving us to ponder the mystery behind this memorial record dedicated to 'Sharons for everybody.'
first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-14.body_text:
Step into spring with the latest edition of First & Last, a series of mixes providing a glimpse into the world of Japanese private press, or 自主盤, pronounced “jishuban”, which loosely translates to “independent board.” 

Included are tracks from two of our favorite records: Book End, whose band leader was also the leader of Primary Mini-Band, and Naotune Saga. The mix also features Buraiha, a band steeped in the avant-garde Angura movement, along with a group called Sharons Forever which (with no liner notes to guide) offers a poignant tribute, leaving us to ponder the mystery behind this memorial record dedicated to ‘Sharons for everybody.’ Onwards. | t craft

light-pollution-the-roots-of-ambient-jaz.title: Light Pollution: The Roots of Ambient Jazz
light-pollution-the-roots-of-ambient-jaz.album: Light Pollution: The Roots of Ambient Jazz
light-pollution-the-roots-of-ambient-jaz.author: Justin Gage
light-pollution-the-roots-of-ambient-jaz.description: As Aquarium Drunkard recently reported, ambient appears to be the shape of jazz to come. The newest new thing is cross-pollinating with electronics and minimalism, new age and drone. But even these currents have a history. We dove deep into our favorite space jazz of yesteryear, and put together a mixtape for your astral travelling pleasure.
light-pollution-the-roots-of-ambient-jaz.body_text:
Fittingly, “He Loved Him Madly” was one of the final pieces Miles Davis recorded in the studio before his five-year retirement from music to an isolated life in his West 77th Street townhouse that revolved around sex, drugs, booze, illness and hallucination. Recorded in June 1974, “He Loved Him Madly” was Miles’s threnody for his friend and idol Duke Ellington, who had died of lung cancer in late May—a tribute to Duke’s signature farewell at the end of each performance, We love you madly. But at over half an hour long, and wrought with liturgical organ, Dominique Gaumont’s haunted guitar playing and a spectral flute solo by Dave Liebman, “He Loved Him Madly” may well have been a dirge for the age of jazz. It certainly signaled the beginning of the end of Miles’s electric era. (Miles would subsequently record two final tracks in the fall, which appeared, alongside “Madly” and some earlier material on Get Up With It in late 1974. He would continue to perform live through the summer of 1975.) And it may well have marked the furthest reaches of what Miles Davis could do with jazz. The virtual negation of the squalling, electric maximalism that characterized his live albums from this period, here was a sonically reduced jazz overtaken by vast, empty space. It is as if the coolness that Miles himself had imparted to jazz decades earlier was now freezing to death. That the most important acolyte of “He Loved Him Madly” was arguably the British art rocker and experimental composer Brian Eno, who cited the work as a touchstone during his initial ambient phase in the late 1970s and early 80s, underscores the extent to which Miles had passed beyond jazz.

As Scott McDowell recently pointed out in his excellent reportage on the shape of jazz to come, this once-outré mode of ambient jazz is now at the vanguard of the genre. McDowell described the reigning sound he heard at the NYC Winter Jazz Fest this past January as “abstract, pretty, burbling along in loose conglomerations of synths and ‘organic’ instrumentation.” We can perhaps be thankful that they aren’t calling it post-jazz, but the name would not be inapposite. After all, jazz is re-staging many of the same sonic encounters that characterized post-rock a quarter century ago—cross-pollinating with electronics and minimalism, new age and drone.

But, as T.S. Eliot pointed out, genuinely new art never merely follows from its predecessors. Something worthy and interesting appears in the contemporary world, and it demands for itself a genealogy. The past must be revisited and reappraised—sometimes rearranged—to account for a startling new present. Tradition is therefore never merely the inert historical backdrop of the present; it is constantly being remade as new work requisitions a lineage by which it might be made sense of. Eighties college rock alters the meaning and place of the jangly, mordant early 70s also-rans of Big Star. The coming of techno and ambient house changes our apprehension of what were once anomalous prog and krautrock tangents like Rainbow Dome Musick and E2-E4. New art warps history around itself; it bends time and space toward it like an immense star.

And so it is with the current moment in jazz. There is the feathery new-age adjacency of the Los Angeles scene around Carlos Niño and Nate Mercereau; or the washed-out, woozy electronics of Sam Gendel; the global pulsations of the Natural Information Society; the gorgeous, sepia-toned heartbreak of the minimalist Lemon Quartet; the dreamy synthesizer psychedelia of Nala Sinephro; the fourth world flute excursions of renegade hip-hop icon Andre 3000; or the doomed groove of Oren Ambarchi, Johan Berthling and Andreas Werliin’s Ghosted trio. The coming of ambient jazz invites us to consider how exactly we got here. And so we did.

The current sounds claim an unlikely ancestry in the funereal fusion of “He Loved Him Madly” and in the quieter moments of Sun Ra; in the synthesized space jazz textures of the extended Mwandishi universe—Hancock, Henderson, Maupin; in the otherworldly, minimalist collaborations of Terry Riley and Don Cherry; in spacious European chamber jazz experiments on the early ECM; and in the smeared, fourth world atmospherics of Jon Hassell. Electronics and acoustics, Moog and flute, drone and funk, are all summoned to presence. North America, South America, Europe and Asia all have a say. The music mostly hails from the 1970s, the era of jazz’s maximal openness to alien sounds, but there are a few outliers from the 80s as well. What unites these disparate pieces is a certain, for lack of a better word, spaciness in the sound.

Indeed, it is remarkable how many of the tracks collected here reference outer space in their titles. Henderson’s “Galaxy.” Ornette Coleman’s “Space Church.” Chivo Borraro’s “Blues for a Cosmonaut.” Elvin Jones’s “Moon Dance,” and so on. Before anyone had the term ambient, artists often simply pointed to the stars to tell us what kind of music we were hearing. All this old space music made me think about light pollution. There’s a tradition there, obscured by the bright lights—like the parts of the night sky we can no longer see. | b sirota

pulp-jazz-twenty-first-century-groove-mu.title: Pulp Jazz: Twenty-First Century Groove Music (A Mixtape)
pulp-jazz-twenty-first-century-groove-mu.album: Pulp Jazz: Twenty-First Century Groove Music (A Mixtape)
pulp-jazz-twenty-first-century-groove-mu.author: Justin Gage
pulp-jazz-twenty-first-century-groove-mu.description: Pulp Jazz draws on long-traduced, sometimes crassly commercial, musical forms—jazz-funk, exotica, new age, sci-fi schlock, lounge music and library—and channels it all into deeply funky, low-key psychedelic groove music. More than that, like the best pulp, it somehow comes out sexy as hell, slinky and dangerous. Aquarium Drunkard has been here for it. The world could stand to be a shade groovier. And when we were asked for a mixtape of the primo stuff, we were more than happy to oblige. It’s what we do. Let’s hope this fresh wave of fusion doesn’t reignite the jazz wars of old. But we’re down to fight if it comes to that.
pulp-jazz-twenty-first-century-groove-mu.body_text:
Last year, I found myself wondering where we would be if heads in the early nineties had gotten into Gábor Szabó instead of John Fahey. Obviously, it never would have happened. Folks at the end of history were looking for rootedness and authenticity, and Blind Joe Death unveiled a strange and haunted American past yawning open beneath our feet like a grave. Just three years Fahey’s senior, Szabó, by contrast, was a mod emblem of jet-setting grooviness. A prodigious talent on guitar, he applied his craft to gypsy jazz and lightweight psychedelia, bossa nova and pop raga. At the turn of the millennium, that kind of hip, beaded cosmopolitanism was a punchline. But now, I am not so sure. Guitar soli primitivism has been running on fumes for a good while now; and suddenly there are flutes and vibes and Fender Rhodes everywhere. Elegant funkiness is on the rise again. And they’re making jazz nowadays (or something like jazz) with amplified horns and Danelectro sitar solos and banks of vintage synthesizers, all redolent a lost era of mid-century sonic excess. Maybe we’re finally sick to death of austerity.

I called it pulp jazz a couple of times, not because it was cheap or disposable, but because it was so immediately gratifying. It draws on long-traduced, sometimes crassly commercial, musical forms—jazz-funk, exotica, new age, sci-fi schlock, lounge music and library—and channels it all into deeply funky, low-key psychedelic groove music. More than that, like the best pulp, it somehow comes out sexy as hell, slinky and dangerous. Aquarium Drunkard has been here for it. The world could stand to be a shade groovier. And when we were asked for a mixtape of the primo stuff, we were more than happy to oblige. It’s what we do.

Let’s hope this fresh wave of fusion doesn’t reignite the jazz wars of old. But we’re down to fight if it comes to that. | b sirota

astral-loitering-excursions-in-new-age-1.title: Astral Loitering: Excursions In New Age, 1970-1989
astral-loitering-excursions-in-new-age-1.album: Astral Loitering: Excursions In New Age, 1970-1989
astral-loitering-excursions-in-new-age-1.author: Justin Gage
astral-loitering-excursions-in-new-age-1.description: New age was, in many ways, a confluence of developments across genres: prog and krautrock, electronic and space music, ambient and fourth world, modern classical, spacious ECM-style Euro-jazz. All of them were cultivating their own approaches to paradise music. What tips any of these experiments into new age may have been, more than anything else, a vibe—an earnestness about the spiritual potential of the music being made.
astral-loitering-excursions-in-new-age-1.body_text:
New age music has never been cool. This is not a knock; it is simply a statement of fact. Coolness, at least in its initial cultural formulation, requires a certain measure of detachment and imperturbability, a studied carelessness in the face of the competing intensities of contemporary life. To get worked up about work or religion or politics or romance was to lend these things more credence than they could possibly deserve. In disinterest, it was hoped, there would be honesty, if not freedom.

But new age, by contrast, radiates warmth. It is uncool, almost by definition. New age lends credence to everything—not just the patchwork of non-Western religious traditions it has long draped itself in, but flying saucers and angels and crystals and, for some reason, dolphins, and, above all, the healing force of music itself. Even at its most sonically reduced (“quiet music,” as the composer Steve Roach dubbed it), new age cannot contain its affection for the universe. It believes in believing. It is enthusiastic in the original sense of the term: en + theos, the god within, a spirit bubbling over.

When the great Greek-born, Sausalito-based new age composer Iasos died at the beginning of this year, I re-watched that wonderful 1979 documentary about him that has been floating around YouTube for some time. And this time I kept noticing the way Iasos giggles. “I realize this is hard for you to believe,” Iasos laughs, as he explains to the interviewer the nature of the being from whom he channels his music. He emits a single shrill ha! as he informs the interviewer that the so-called “paradise music” he manifests might have “sixteen thousand melodies at a time.” Iasos wasn’t crazy; he knew what he was saying sounded outlandish. He laughed because he was unabashed by it, where one might have expected him to be otherwise. He was unembarrassed by joy.

This brazen, unapologetic ardor is what sets much of new age off from the other musical genres in its vicinity. Indeed, the more you listen to new age the more it tends to evaporate as a distinct and recognizable musical form. (Which is just as well since its many of its most famous practitioners disclaimed the label altogether.) At its margins, new age was continuous with nearly every other mode of exploratory music in the 1970s. The mix that follows aims to capture something of this continuum. Much of this sound evolved out of the broader world of European progressive rock. Tim Blake, for instance, with whom the mixtape starts, was a veteran of the Radio Gnome Invisible-era version of Gong. And Vangelis, captured here in a 1971 London jam session, had just emerged from the Greek expat prog band Aphrodite’s Child. The German sitarist Al Gromer Khan, meanwhile, played on some of the classic Popol Vuh albums of the later 1970s. And the Frenchman Bernard Xolotl is often best thought of as a Gallic Ash Ra Tempel. The Belgian Joel Vandroogenbroeck parlayed his time in the krautrock-inspired psychedelic outfit Brainticket into a career in library music.

Others here were clearly working through the impact of minimalism. The Texan J.D. Emmanuel, for instance, is clearly hammering out a workingman’s Persian Surgery Dervishes. While Ariel Kalma and Büdi Siebert are plainly drawing on Terry Riley’s circular, time-lagged saxophone. And the marimbas and vibes on Brian Slawson’s percussion workout achieve a Reich-ian momentum. Still others were cooking on the fringes of 1970s jazz. Steve Kindler, for instance, had played violin in the second incarnation of the Mahavishnu Orchestra. And Jon Bernoff and Marcus Allen’s duet bears more than a passing resemblance to Chick Corea and Gary Burton’s great Crystal Vision sessions. The duo Emerald Web, the Berlin-based Achim Gieseler, who performed as Jakino, and the California synthesist Sanford Ponder were all building on the ‘fourth world’ fusion sound pioneered by Jon Hassell. William Eaton, meanwhile, sounds like a refugee from the Takoma school of American primitivism hiding out in the Arizona desert. New age was, in many ways, a confluence of developments across genres: prog and krautrock, electronic and space music, ambient and fourth world, modern classical, spacious ECM-style Euro-jazz. All of them were cultivating their own approaches to paradise music. What tips any of these experiments into new age may have been, more than anything else, a vibe—an earnestness about the spiritual potential of the music being made.

Ironically, the utter guilelessness of new age music may have helped degrade this most ethereally lofty sound into crass utilitarianism. No other genre of music bothers to spell out its purpose on the package. But straplines like “Timeless Music for Meditation and Healing” or “Induces an Altered State of Consciousness” routinely appear on new age albums. It is not so much that their promises were laughably overwrought but, rather, that art shouldn’t promise to do anything. Poetry, Auden told us, makes nothing happen. And music, I suspect, does even less. The evangelism of the musicians (or, increasingly, new age label marketing departments) about the curative properties of their work permitted their weird, beautiful music to become mere therapy—a treatment for the innumerable symptoms of modern malaise. The whole thing became just another American medicine show. I don’t blame them for trying to sell records. But I do wish they hadn’t mistaken art for analgesics.    

New age music, it turns out, was something of a canary in the coalmine. The evolution of new age from a music of often surpassing strangeness and sometimes unendurable expanse into a bland fixture of bourgeois wellness heralded the dispiriting way for the entire counterculture. As goes new age, so, eventually, followed yoga and organic produce, juice cleanses and microdosed psychedelics. Mindfulness is now something human resources recommends in lieu of a decent healthcare plan.

Still, new age was once wooly and weird and, occasionally, ecstatic. Iasos laughed, unembarrassed by joy. We can pass a few hours there. We’re not all unhappy. | b sirota | art joseph parker

the-secret-hemisphere-new-age-fusion-and.title: The Secret Hemisphere: New Age, Fusion and Fourth World, 1970-2002
the-secret-hemisphere-new-age-fusion-and.album: The Secret Hemisphere: New Age, Fusion and Fourth World, 1970-2002
the-secret-hemisphere-new-age-fusion-and.author: Justin Gage
the-secret-hemisphere-new-age-fusion-and.description: Fourth world music belongs to an occult geography. This is by design. When the late trumpeter and composer Jon Hassell coined the term, he conceived of it as “an attempt to create a kind of musical scenery which is not entirely ‘primitive,’ not entirely ‘future’ but someplace impossible to locate either chronologically or geographically.” There is no atlas to the fourth world. The best that we can offer is something like a star chart. You may have to draw the constellations yourselves, out of lines connecting ECM jazz to phase music, woolly hippie cult rock to a thousand forgotten new age cassettes. Navigate by those for now. You’ll get somewhere eventually. Here's a compass.
the-secret-hemisphere-new-age-fusion-and.body_text:
Fourth world music belongs to an occult geography. This is by design. When the late trumpeter and composer Jon Hassell coined the term, he conceived of it as “an attempt to create a kind of musical scenery which is not entirely ‘primitive,’ not entirely ‘future’ but someplace impossible to locate either chronologically or geographically.” Like its unacknowledged predecessor, exotica, fourth world conjures with other continents, but they are largely hypothetical. The music sounds transoceanic, but not from any place in particular. After all, the fourth world was always a sonic conjecture. Possible musics, as Hassell and Brian Eno subtitled their initial collaboration. The legendary punk producer Craig Leon was perhaps even closer to the mark when he whimsically referred to it as interplanetary folk music. It is the world music of a surrogate earth. And as such, it is utopian in the literal sense of the word: from nowhere. Or, at least, from nowhere exactly.

The idea of a music impossible to place has, in large measure, prevented fourth world music from ever being fully swallowed up by any of the more recognizable genres upon which it tends to draw. It is too ostentatiously synthetic for world music, too aggressively weird for new age, too repetitious for jazz, and altogether too spiritually dippy for serious minimalism. But one could find their way into it from any of these musical territories. Or from the ethnic fringes of krautrock; from Berlin school synthesizer fantasias or even from the (objectionably named) tribal ambient of some archaic 1990s chillout room. Like all truly hidden places, you don’t go looking for it. You find yourself there.

But wherever it is, it is teeming with life. Perhaps more than the marriage of synthesizers to traditional and non-Western musical forms that usually denotes fourth world, the sound often possesses a burbling, mutating vitality. It bubbles and swarms. The ambient composer Robert Rich coined the unprepossessing term glurp to indicate “the organic manifestations of liquid, squelching lifeforms” inhabiting his music. This strange, squirming abundance distinguishes fourth world music from the vast drones and abstractions that otherwise characterize new age and space music.

I’ve been thinking about a kid whose mind got switched on by New Blue Sun. Where would she go from there? Where would the alien wheeze of Andre 3000’s improbable wind synthesizer first lead them? There is no atlas to the fourth world. The best that we can offer is something like a star chart. You may have to draw the constellations yourselves, out of lines connecting ECM jazz to phase music, woolly hippie cult rock to a thousand forgotten new age cassettes. Navigate by those for now. You’ll get somewhere eventually. | b sirota

first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-12.body_text:
Welcome to the twelfth installment of First & Last, a series of mixes providing a glimpse into the world of Japanese private press, or 自主盤, pronounced “jishuban”, which loosely translates to “independent board.”

This latest medley unfolds like a muted sonnet, a poignant exploration of singer-songwriter purity that captures the essence of musical solitude. As autumn fades into the quiet embrace of winter, immerse yourself in the hushed tones of acoustic introspection, where each note invokes the stillness of the changing seasons. | t craft

anatolian-psych-out-volume-one.title: Anatolian Psych Out: Volume One
anatolian-psych-out-volume-one.author: Justin Gage
anatolian-psych-out-volume-one.description: As Western rock music dominated the global airwaves of the 1960s, perhaps no region adapted this music into such a rich and swirling stew as did Turkey. Mixing the heavy riffs of Zeppelin and the Stones with traditional Anatolian folk melodies and instrumentation, the Turkish psych rock of the golden era (1960s-80s) surely represents some of the most gloriously untethered interpretations of the classic rock canon.
anatolian-psych-out-volume-one.body_text:
As Western rock music dominated the global airwaves of the 1960s, perhaps no region adapted this music into such a rich and swirling stew as did Turkey. Mixing the heavy riffs of Zeppelin and the Stones with traditional Anatolian folk melodies and instrumentation, the Turkish psych rock of the golden era (1960s-80s) surely represents some of the most gloriously untethered interpretations of the classic rock canon. The following 21 tracks showcase some of the heaviest hitting cuts laid down during that era, in all their weird and wild abandon. | m wright

first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-11.body_text:
Welcome to the eleventh installment of First & Last, a series of mixes providing a glimpse into the world of Japanese private press, or 自主盤, pronounced “jishuban”, which loosely translates to “independent board.”

Mirroring the vibrant shift in seasons, allow the following mix to soundtrack the transition from summer’s warmth to autumn’s embrace. Highlights include a favorite from Abeichizoku (reissue dropping early November), alongside a Tetsumi Hibino gem from a compilation we recently gripped. Last, but not least, we hop across the pond to one of Kikuko Murakami’s two LA based bands, Silver Lake.

aquarium-drunkard-presents-tele-music-mi.title: Aquarium Drunkard Presents: Tele Music Mix Volume 1
aquarium-drunkard-presents-tele-music-mi.album: Aquarium Drunkard Presents: Tele Music Mix Volume 1
aquarium-drunkard-presents-tele-music-mi.author: Justin Gage
aquarium-drunkard-presents-tele-music-mi.description: Back in the deep, bleak, pandemic dog days, Be With Records struck a deal with the legendary Parisian library music catalog, Tele Music to reissue the very best, most sought-after LPs in the catalog. With much of this music now commercially available for the first time, the series of albums recently reissued presents an expansive grip of heavy funk, drama-jazz, deep disco, synth-wave, psych-rock, cosmic soul and beyond.
aquarium-drunkard-presents-tele-music-mi.body_text:
Back in the deep, bleak, pandemic dog days, Be With Records struck a deal with the legendary Parisian library music catalog, Tele Music to reissue the very best, most sought-after LPs in the catalog. With much of this music now commercially available for the first time, the series of albums recently reissued presents an expansive grip of heavy funk, drama-jazz, deep disco, synth-wave, psych-rock, cosmic soul and beyond.

Following several years of intense work producing these – so far – 12 vital reissues, Be With founder Rob Butler decided to helm a selection of the cream of the Tele Music catalog for this exclusive Aquarium Drunkard mix. Cut live in one take, using all vinyl at Be With’s Manchester HQ, he selected some of Tele Music’s most crucial tracks for an incisive 80 minute DJ mix.

We appreciate it can be overwhelming if you’re new to these incredible cult classics so we’ve picked out all the fire and synthesized them into a flowing selection for this mix. In fact, there’s so much unmitigated heat, we’re going to have to do this mix in 3 parts. Here’s Part I…”

Aquarium Drunkard Presents: Tele Music Mix Volume 1

Welcome to the eighth installment of First & Last, a series of mixes providing a glimpse into the world of Japanese private press, or 自主盤, pronounced “jishuban”, which loosely translates to “independent board.” 

First & Last: Japanese Private Press, Vol. 8

This eighth entry spans the early 70s to early 80s. Like most bedroom pop, it focuses on love, and the self-serious tribulations of growing older. Some of the album and track titles roughly translate to “At a Distance, I Love you”, “Farewell to Love”, “Impression Love”, “One Line Love Song”, “My Soliloquy of Solitary”, “Elegy of a 20 year old”, “Bye-Bye Wisdom”, and ”I have no use for anyone who throws this LP away.” A perfect harbinger of Spring. | t craft

first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-7.album: First & Last: Japanese Private Press, Vol. 7
first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-7.author: Justin Gage
first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-7.description: Welcome to the seventh installment of First & Last, a series of mixes providing a glimpse into the world of Japanese private press, or 自主盤, pronounced “jishuban”, which loosely translates to “independent board.”
first-last-japanese-private-press-vol-7.body_text:
The seventh installment of First & Last is brought to you by guest curator, Johan Nilsson. Johan and I became friends through our shared loved of collecting rare and unheard 70s records from Japan. Earlier this year we started our own record label together under the same moniker as this mix series; First & Last Records. The label will focus on reissuing private press records from Japan …our first release will be announced soon, with many more to follow. 

First & Last: Japanese Private Press, Vol. 7

本田大助 (Daisuke Honda) – 夏 (1977)⁣
ババロア (Bavarois) – 貴方が瞳を閉じた時に (1973)⁣
ジョニーアップルシード (Johnny Appleseed) – 私は感じやすい女 (1973)⁣
佐崎潤一 (Junichi Sazaki) – 心 Part I (1978)⁣
百舌 (Mozu) – そんな暮し (1977)⁣
劇団・無頼派 (Gekidan Buraiha) – Untitled (1975?)⁣
ふくやまゆきお (Yukio Fukuyama) – スミのいれずみ (1973)⁣
Love Orphan – このドアを開けたら (1972?)⁣
吉野明彦とザ・シャーマン (Akihiko Yoshino & The Shaman) – 酔いどれヘリコプター (1973?)⁣
フォーク・キャンディーズ (Folk Candies) – 黒いカラス (1973?)⁣
Yokohama – 恋する乙女 (1972?)⁣
卒業生と在校生 (Sotsugyousei & Zaikousei) – 松本通り (197?)

Johan’s notes: In trying to write about these records, or as Tyler and I are trying to do, reissue them, the biggest hurdle is trying to obtain any useful information. This mix starts out with a song by Daisuke Honda, from his 1977 album Love Songs, which is one of my favorite private press records to come out of Japan. We tried hard to find him in hopes of reissuing the album but unfortunately, we’ve had no luck. Like most copies, mine has no cover, let alone an insert. Many of these records were only made in 50-100 copies and often much fewer covers were created because they were handmade or because of budget limitations. We only know a cover for the Honda LP exist because it’s pictured in the book Rock Diving Magazine vol. 7 (1999), from a copy supposedly scored in a dumpster. Missing information can also lead to misinformation, as has happened with the duo Mozu on the mix. Their first album was commonly mistaken to have been made by a band called Age (and referred to as such in the Wonder Ground Record Guide Book (2020)) until a collector uncovered a copy with a cover and shared it. I’ve listed the penultimate track on this mix as being by an act called Yokohama but that’s only because the single has no cover and that’s all it says on the labels, and I’ve never seen another copy.

But that’s also a part of what makes it entrancing to collecting this music, it’s an endless mystery, from finding something unknown to trying to learn more about it. Most of the records on this mix were bought on a whim or because of some vague connection that could be made. I bought the Akihiko Yoshino & The Shaman EP based the fact that it was made by the collectible Nihon C.A.C Record label (who the same year put out the wonderful Yuji Ikeda album) and the Folk Candies EP caught my attention because of the insert where one of the members lists Happy End, as I think his favorite band. Most of these tracks have stories like that, loose threads wrapped in enigmas, pressed on polyvinyl chloride.

This mix is a bit heavy on the folky side, what’s often called ‘acid folk’ in Japan even though it is not really psychedelic. Feels right for the season though and fitting for a nation that rejected daylight savings time, imposed by the American occupiers post-World War II, to remain in constant wintertime.

 

 
 Posted on December 22, 2022August 28, 2025First & Last Japan Mixtapes

groove-orient-south-asian-elements-in-ps.title: Groove Orient: South Asian Elements in Psychedelic Jazz
groove-orient-south-asian-elements-in-ps.album: Groove Orient: South Asian Elements in Psychedelic Jazz
groove-orient-south-asian-elements-in-ps.author: Justin Gage
groove-orient-south-asian-elements-in-ps.description: Jazz's engagement with South Asian musical ideas and instruments in the 1960s and 70s didn't just make 'spiritual' or 'world' jazz. Out of the extraordinary variety of jazz experiments with Indian musical traditions came all kinds of funky, soulful, groovy, exploratory and just plain far out sounds. We collected some of our favorites.
groove-orient-south-asian-elements-in-ps.body_text:
The late great jazz guitarist Pat Martino’s Baiyina (The Clear Evidence) resurfaced on vinyl this month. The album hadn’t been reissued since its first and only CD pressing in the late 1980s, and the original LPs are now rarer than hen’s teeth. Thankfully, the new edition retains the original album’s mildly hallucinogenic cover art; as well as its somewhat ridiculous strapline: A psychedelic excursion through the magical mysteries of the Koran. If the day-glo coloring and eastern spirituality didn’t place the album squarely in the middle of 1968, the transparent Beatles reference cinches it. Baiyina appeared about six months after Magical Mystery Tour, and exuded the profound anxiety of jazz’s displacement from the whole of youth culture. Outrageously, the 1960s counterculture presented a version of hip which, by and large, did not encompass jazz. And the electricity and paisley and mysticism that came to characterize jazz in the late 60s and 70s constituted, in no small part, its bid for inclusion.

The tabla beats and droning tamboura that opens Martino’s Baiyina situates us right in the midst of what Ravi Shankar called “the sitar explosion” in western music. But if Martino was as guilty as his rock contemporaries in using Indian instrumentation as an exotic shorthand for spiritual depth, he still managed to pull off a fantastic slice of soul jazz here. Indeed, what is wonderful about Baiyina is that the south Asian elements and spiritual pretensions do not suddenly turn Martino into a would-be acid rock divinity like John McLaughlin. He remains the nimble and spidery soul jazz groover he was on all those killer early Prestige cuts—under his own name as well as his work with Eric Kloss and Brother Jack McDuff. (And it is Martino’s clean, but thrillingly unpredictable guitar lines from this era that remain the ideal gateway drug for Deadheads looking to get into jazz.) The drones season the groove, but they do not overtake it.

Baiyina (The Clear Evidence) is an outstanding reminder that south Asian elements could do all kinds of work in sixties and seventies jazz. Their integration into jazz (and related forms like fusion and krautrock), whether superficial or substantive, did not necessarily signal an attempted raga. And they did not turn everything they touched into rarefied “spiritual” or “world” jazz. What follows is a compilation which attempts to set Martino’s work against the extraordinary variety of jazz experiments with Indian musical traditions and instruments in the 1960s and 70s—by south Asian, European and American musicians alike, often in exciting collaborations. Some of it still reeks of incense. But there’s all kinds of funky, soulful, exploratory and just plain weird stuff to be heard.

Hopefully, some of it is new and all of it is surprising. Miles Davis’s limpid “Yaphet” from the Bitches Brew sessions, for instance, does not resemble the dense and noisy integration of Indian instruments that characterized the On the Corner material a few years later. And one forgets how wickedly weird and funky Alice Coltrane’s cover of her late husband’s signature “A Love Supreme” gets in the middle. Perennial pop trend-chaser Herbie Mann makes an absolute monster out of an eastern-tinged Beatles cover. And the Oregon tune cooks way hotter than their proto-New Age meditations elsewhere might lead one to expect. The slinky smoke of Amancio D’Silva’s “What Maria Sees” is a necessary reminder that everything on his recently reissued ‘lost’ album Konkan Dance is an indispensable late-night burner. And don’t sleep on Volker Kriegel’s sitar playing on his own cut and with the Dave Pike Set. Remember, none of it has to be enlightening; it just has to sound good. | b sirota

saturnalia-deep-jazz-for-long-nights-196.title: Saturnalia: Deep Jazz for Long Nights, 1969-1980
saturnalia-deep-jazz-for-long-nights-196.album: Saturnalia: Deep Jazz for Long Nights, 1969-1980
saturnalia-deep-jazz-for-long-nights-196.author: Justin Gage
saturnalia-deep-jazz-for-long-nights-196.description: The days are growing shorter and the holidays are upon us. In the interest of making everyone's lives slightly easier this season, we returned to the golden era of electric jazz and put together a long compilation of cocktail funk and rare groovers to spin for a house full of family and friends. Press play and let it spin. One less thing to worry about.
saturnalia-deep-jazz-for-long-nights-196.body_text:
The days are growing shorter and the holidays are upon us. And before long, you might find yourself with a house full of friends, family and neighbors. In a perfect world, your dearest relations have the same expansive, searching taste in music that you do. But here on earth, you’re hard pressed to find a single one among them to whom you might willingly cede control of the stereo. Let them mix drinks. Let them tend the fire. They can even keep an eye on the roast. But it’s your house, you pick the tunes.

In the interest of making your lives slightly easier this season, and in the spirit of our previous dives into the intercontinental electric jazz underground, we put together a compilation of rare groovers to spin for a house full of people—people who, we assume, do not read Aquarium Drunkard regularly. Press play and let it go. One less thing to worry about. No seismic fusion here; no weird Teutonic incantations. We returned to the golden era of electrified jazz, with a mind toward keeping it tight and groovy. That is not to say featherweight or crassly commercial, but simply deep and funky jazz that the whole family can enjoy. Flutes and Fender Rhodes abound. The guitars are trim and limber. The synthesizers are tasteful. The rhythms undeniable. There’s a few pop covers here and some lesser known interpretations of modern jazz standards. In the season of goodwill toward mankind, we kept the mix appropriately global: France, Germany, Japan, Switzerland, Finland, Guadeloupe, Belgium, Brazil, the United States and elsewhere. We tried to find that sweet spot where those paying attention bop along, while those otherwise preoccupied don’t object. You might even turn on an aunt or a cousin when they ask what’s on the hi-fi. Your stoner uncle will nod knowingly.

There’s no grand historical thesis here. We just wanted music to shake a cocktail to, to make small talk with co-workers, to keep at bay the hideous culture war blow-ups poised to blacken our every attempt at sociability this holiday season. Perhaps we are being naïve. But everything on here smokes: the elegant lite funk exercises no less than the knottier fusion workouts. And we are, after all, idealists. We believe that, in the end, a killer soundtrack helps keep the darkness from all our doors. | b sirota
